sources.sourceName: OpenStax College Physics for AP® Courses 2e, Ch. 7 (Work, Energy, and Energy Resources) — Test Prep for AP® Courses
sources.sourceLicense: CC BY-NC-SA 4.0 (OpenStax; live page also posts a no-LLM-training notice — SpeedyCAT use is human-facing, non-AI per PRD)

meta.notes: Physics (CPBS) work/energy items. Stems and four options scraped verbatim from OpenStax College Physics for AP Courses 2e Ch. 7 'Test Prep for AP Courses' single-best-answer multiple-choice items. OpenStax publishes the answer key to instructors only, so each correctAnswer was independently verified from the same CC BY-NC-SA source text or by direct calculation (answerProvenance = verified-from-source-text). Two items (cpbs-xp040, cpbs-xp041) reference figures whose content (a force-vs-distance profile; spring compression) is fully described in the question text, so no image is needed. Ambiguous items, items needing an un-capturable data table/graph, and free-response items were skipped. Where g was needed, calculations used 9.8 m/s² except where the source's own answer choices imply g = 10 m/s² (noted per item).

questions.stem: A crane lifts construction materials from the ground to an elevation of 60 m. Over the first 10 m, the motor linearly increases the force it exerts from 0 to 10 kN. It exerts that constant 10 kN force for the next 40 m, and then winds the force down to 0 N again over the last 10 m. What is the total work done on the construction materials?
questions.explanation: Work is the area under the force-vs-distance graph: a triangle for the first 10 m (½ × 10 m × 10 kN = 50 kJ), a rectangle for the middle 40 m (40 m × 10 kN = 400 kJ), and a triangle for the last 10 m (50 kJ). Total = 50 + 400 + 50 = 500 kJ.
questions.sourceName: OpenStax College Physics for AP® Courses 2e, Ch. 7 — Test Prep for AP® Courses
questions.sourceLicense: CC BY-NC-SA 4.0 (OpenStax; live page also posts a no-LLM-training notice — SpeedyCAT use is human-facing, non-AI per PRD)
questions.notes: Based on OpenStax Figure 7.45; the force profile is fully described in the question text (no image needed). OpenStax withholds the public answer key; answer independently verified by computing the area under the force-distance graph.

hints.prompt: The work done by a variable force over a distance equals:

choices.label: A
choices.text: the area under the force-vs-distance graph

choices.label: B
choices.text: the final force times the total distance

choices.label: C
choices.text: the average distance times the time

choices.label: D
choices.text: the peak force divided by the distance

hints.rationale: Work is the area under the force-vs-distance curve.

hints.prompt: The profile is a triangle (0→10 kN over 10 m), a rectangle (10 kN over 40 m), then a triangle (10 kN→0 over 10 m). How do you get the total work?

choices.label: B
choices.text: multiply 10 kN by the full 60 m

hints.rationale: Total work is the sum of the two triangular areas and the rectangular area under the graph.

hints.prompt: Treating the force as the full 10 kN over the entire 60 m (ignoring the ramp-up and ramp-down) overcounts the work, and 18 MJ is off by orders of magnitude. Which pair of options can be eliminated?
hints.rationale: 600 kJ ignores the triangular ramps (overcount) and 18 MJ is far too large, so both are eliminated.

questions.stem: You compress a spring by a distance x, and then release it. Next you compress the same spring by 2x. How much work did you do the second time compared with the first?
questions.explanation: The work to compress a spring (its stored elastic potential energy) is ½kx², which is proportional to the square of the compression. Doubling the compression multiplies the work by 2² = 4.
questions.sourceName: OpenStax College Physics for AP® Courses 2e, Ch. 7 — Test Prep for AP® Courses
questions.sourceLicense: CC BY-NC-SA 4.0 (OpenStax; live page also posts a no-LLM-training notice — SpeedyCAT use is human-facing, non-AI per PRD)
questions.notes: Stem references Figure 7.10(c); rephrased to be self-contained since the answer follows from the spring energy relation ½kx². OpenStax withholds the public answer key; answer independently verified.

hints.prompt: The work needed to compress a spring by a distance x (its stored elastic energy) is:
hints.rationale: Spring energy is ½kx², proportional to the square of the compression.

hints.prompt: A student reasons that compressing twice as far requires twice the work. Why is this linear reasoning wrong for a spring?

choices.label: A
choices.text: spring energy depends on x², not x, so the scaling is not linear

choices.label: B
choices.text: it is actually correct—work is linear in x

choices.label: C
choices.text: because the spring constant k also doubles

hints.rationale: Spring energy ∝ x², so the work does not merely double when x doubles.

hints.prompt: Compressing the spring FARTHER must require MORE work, not less or the same. Which pair of options can be eliminated?

choices.label: B
choices.text: 'Twice as much' and 'Four times as much'

hints.rationale: More compression means more work, so 'half' and 'the same' are eliminated.

questions.stem: A toy car is going around a loop-the-loop. Gravity ____ the kinetic energy on the upward side of the loop, ____ the kinetic energy at the very top, and ____ the kinetic energy on the downward side of the loop.

choices.label: A
choices.text: increases, decreases, has no effect on

choices.label: B
choices.text: decreases, has no effect on, increases

choices.label: C
choices.text: increases, has no effect on, decreases

choices.label: D
choices.text: decreases, increases, has no effect on

questions.explanation: Gravity does negative work while the car climbs (KE decreases), zero work at the very top where the velocity is horizontal and gravity is perpendicular to the motion (no effect), and positive work as the car descends (KE increases).
questions.sourceName: OpenStax College Physics for AP® Courses 2e, Ch. 7 — Test Prep for AP® Courses
questions.sourceLicense: CC BY-NC-SA 4.0 (OpenStax; live page also posts a no-LLM-training notice — SpeedyCAT use is human-facing, non-AI per PRD)
questions.notes: Stem + 4 options scraped verbatim. OpenStax withholds the public answer key; answer independently verified from the sign of the work done by gravity.

hints.prompt: Gravity does positive work (raising KE) when motion has a downward component and negative work (lowering KE) when it has an upward component. When motion is exactly horizontal, gravity does:
hints.rationale: Gravity does no work when the displacement is perpendicular to it (horizontal motion).

hints.prompt: At the very top of the loop the car's velocity is horizontal while gravity points straight down. What is gravity's effect on the KE right there?

choices.label: A
choices.text: no effect (gravity is perpendicular to the motion)

hints.rationale: At the top the motion is horizontal and gravity is vertical, so gravity does no work there.

hints.prompt: You established that at the TOP gravity has NO effect on kinetic energy. Which pair of options can be eliminated because their middle blank (the top) is nonzero?

choices.label: A
choices.text: 'increases, decreases, has no effect on' and 'decreases, increases, has no effect on'

choices.label: B
choices.text: 'decreases, has no effect on, increases' and 'increases, has no effect on, decreases'

choices.label: C
choices.text: 'increases, decreases, has no effect on' and 'increases, has no effect on, decreases'

choices.label: D
choices.text: 'decreases, increases, has no effect on' and 'decreases, has no effect on, increases'

hints.rationale: Both of those put a nonzero effect at the top, contradicting that gravity does no work there, so they are eliminated.

questions.stem: A model drag car is accelerated along its track from rest by a motor with a force of 75 N, but there is a drag force of 30 N due to the track. What is the kinetic energy after 2 m of travel?
questions.explanation: By the work-energy theorem, the change in kinetic energy equals the net work. The net force is 75 - 30 = 45 N, so over 2 m the net work is 45 N × 2 m = 90 J. Starting from rest, the kinetic energy is 90 J.
questions.sourceName: OpenStax College Physics for AP® Courses 2e, Ch. 7 — Test Prep for AP® Courses
questions.sourceLicense: CC BY-NC-SA 4.0 (OpenStax; live page also posts a no-LLM-training notice — SpeedyCAT use is human-facing, non-AI per PRD)
questions.notes: Stem + 4 options scraped verbatim. OpenStax withholds the public answer key; answer independently verified by the work-energy theorem (net force × distance).

hints.prompt: The work–energy theorem says the change in an object's kinetic energy equals:

choices.label: A
choices.text: the NET work done on it (net force × distance)

choices.label: B
choices.text: only the applied force times distance

choices.label: D
choices.text: the drag force times the distance

hints.rationale: ΔKE equals the net work, i.e., net force times distance.

hints.prompt: The motor pushes with 75 N and drag opposes with 30 N. What NET force should you use over the 2 m?
hints.rationale: The net force is the forward force minus the opposing drag: 45 N.

hints.prompt: Drag OPPOSES the motor, so the net force (and thus the work) must be LESS than using the 75 N motor force alone. Which pair of options can be eliminated as too large?
hints.rationale: Using 75 N alone gives 150 J and adding drag (105 N) gives 210 J; since drag reduces the net force, both are too large.

questions.stem: When the force acting on an object is parallel to the direction of motion of the center of mass, the mechanical energy ____. When the force acting on an object is antiparallel to the direction of motion of the center of mass, the mechanical energy ____.
questions.explanation: A force parallel to the motion does positive work, adding mechanical energy; a force antiparallel to the motion does negative work, removing mechanical energy.
questions.sourceName: OpenStax College Physics for AP® Courses 2e, Ch. 7 — Test Prep for AP® Courses
questions.sourceLicense: CC BY-NC-SA 4.0 (OpenStax; live page also posts a no-LLM-training notice — SpeedyCAT use is human-facing, non-AI per PRD)
questions.notes: Stem + 4 options scraped verbatim. OpenStax withholds the public answer key; answer independently verified from the sign of work done by parallel vs antiparallel forces.

hints.prompt: A force does POSITIVE work (adding mechanical energy) when it points:
hints.rationale: A force along the motion does positive work, adding mechanical energy.

hints.prompt: A sled coasting forward is pushed by a force pointing BACKWARD (opposite its motion). Over that push, its mechanical energy:
hints.rationale: A backward (antiparallel) force does negative work, so mechanical energy decreases.

hints.prompt: A force parallel to the motion (the FIRST blank) must INCREASE mechanical energy. Which pair of options can be eliminated because their first blank is 'decreases'?
hints.rationale: A parallel force adds energy, so both options beginning with 'decreases' are eliminated.

questions.stem: A child is pulling two red wagons, with the second one tied to the first by a non-stretching rope. Each wagon has a mass of 10 kg. If the child exerts a force of 30 N for 5.0 m (ignore friction), how much has the kinetic energy of the two-wagon system changed?
questions.explanation: The net external work on the two-wagon system equals the applied force times the distance: 30 N × 5.0 m = 150 J. By the work-energy theorem, the system's kinetic energy increases by 150 J (the internal rope tension does no net work on the system).
questions.sourceName: OpenStax College Physics for AP® Courses 2e, Ch. 7 — Test Prep for AP® Courses
questions.sourceLicense: CC BY-NC-SA 4.0 (OpenStax; live page also posts a no-LLM-training notice — SpeedyCAT use is human-facing, non-AI per PRD)
questions.notes: Stem + 4 options scraped verbatim. OpenStax withholds the public answer key; answer independently verified by the work-energy theorem.

hints.prompt: For a connected system pulled by an external force, the change in the system's kinetic energy equals:

choices.label: A
choices.text: the net EXTERNAL work (external force × distance)

choices.label: B
choices.text: the internal rope tension times distance

hints.rationale: ΔKE of the system equals the net external work; internal forces do no net work.

hints.prompt: The child pulls with 30 N over 5.0 m. Which quantities give the system's change in kinetic energy (the rope between the wagons is internal)?
hints.rationale: Only the external 30 N over 5.0 m does net work; the internal rope tension cancels out.

hints.prompt: The internal rope tension does no NET work, so you should not double the work (one per wagon) nor halve it. Which pair of options can be eliminated?
hints.rationale: Counting 30 N once over 5 m gives the work; doubling it (300 J) or halving it (75 J) misuses the internal tension, so both are eliminated.

questions.stem: A 1.0 kg baseball is flying at 10 m/s. How much kinetic energy does it have, and how much gravitational potential energy?
questions.explanation: Kinetic energy is ½mv² = ½(1.0)(10)² = 50 J. Gravitational potential energy depends on the chosen reference height, which is not given, so it cannot be determined from the information provided.
questions.sourceName: OpenStax College Physics for AP® Courses 2e, Ch. 7 — Test Prep for AP® Courses
questions.sourceLicense: CC BY-NC-SA 4.0 (OpenStax; live page also posts a no-LLM-training notice — SpeedyCAT use is human-facing, non-AI per PRD)
questions.notes: Stem + 4 options scraped verbatim. OpenStax withholds the public answer key; answer independently verified (PE needs a reference height).

hints.prompt: Gravitational potential energy (mgh) can be assigned a value only once you have:

choices.label: A
choices.text: chosen a reference height where PE = 0

hints.rationale: PE depends on a chosen reference height, so a reference is required.

hints.prompt: The baseball's speed (10 m/s) and mass (1.0 kg) are given, but no height or reference. Which energy can you compute and which can't you?

choices.label: A
choices.text: kinetic energy can be computed; potential energy cannot (no reference height)

choices.label: D
choices.text: potential energy can be computed; kinetic energy cannot

hints.rationale: KE = ½mv² needs only mass and speed; PE needs a reference height, which is missing.

hints.prompt: The kinetic energy is definitely computable as ½·1.0·10² = 50 J. Which pair of options can be eliminated because they give the WRONG kinetic energy (the first value)?
hints.rationale: KE is 50 J, so options listing the KE as 10 J or 'unknown' are eliminated.

questions.stem: A 0.305-kg potato has been launched out of a potato cannon at 15.8 m/s. What is its kinetic energy? If you then learn that it is 4.00 m above the ground, what is the total mechanical energy relative to the ground? (Take g = 9.8 m/s².)
questions.explanation: Kinetic energy = ½mv² = ½(0.305)(15.8)² ≈ 38.1 J. Potential energy = mgh = 0.305 × 9.8 × 4.00 ≈ 12.0 J. Total mechanical energy = 38.1 + 12.0 ≈ 50.1 J.
questions.sourceName: OpenStax College Physics for AP® Courses 2e, Ch. 7 — Test Prep for AP® Courses
questions.sourceLicense: CC BY-NC-SA 4.0 (OpenStax; live page also posts a no-LLM-training notice — SpeedyCAT use is human-facing, non-AI per PRD)
questions.notes: Stem + 4 options scraped verbatim. OpenStax withholds the public answer key; answer independently verified by calculation.

hints.prompt: The total mechanical energy of an object is:

choices.label: A
choices.text: the sum of its kinetic energy and potential energy (KE + PE)

hints.rationale: Mechanical energy is KE + PE.

hints.prompt: You compute KE = ½mv² and PE = mgh, then add them. Which pair of formulas is set up correctly here?
hints.rationale: KE = ½mv² and PE = mgh with the given numbers.

hints.prompt: The value 12.0 J is actually the POTENTIAL energy (mgh), not the kinetic energy, so options listing 12.0 J as the KE (first value) are traps. Which pair can be eliminated?
hints.rationale: 12.0 J is the potential energy (mgh), not the kinetic energy, so both options that put 12.0 J first are eliminated.

questions.stem: Two 4.0 kg masses are connected to each other by a spring with a force constant of 25 N/m and a rest length of 1.0 m. If the spring has been compressed to 0.80 m in length and the masses are traveling toward each other at 0.50 m/s each, what is the total energy in the system?
questions.explanation: Spring compression is 1.0 - 0.80 = 0.20 m, so elastic PE = ½k(Δx)² = ½(25)(0.20)² = 0.50 J. Kinetic energy = 2 × ½mv² = 2 × ½(4.0)(0.50)² = 1.0 J. Total = 1.0 + 0.5 = 1.5 J.
questions.sourceName: OpenStax College Physics for AP® Courses 2e, Ch. 7 — Test Prep for AP® Courses
questions.sourceLicense: CC BY-NC-SA 4.0 (OpenStax; live page also posts a no-LLM-training notice — SpeedyCAT use is human-facing, non-AI per PRD)
questions.notes: Stem + 4 options scraped verbatim. OpenStax withholds the public answer key; answer independently verified by calculation.

hints.prompt: The total mechanical energy of a mass–spring system is:

choices.label: A
choices.text: the sum of the kinetic energies plus the elastic potential energy ½k(Δx)²

choices.label: D
choices.text: the spring constant times the speed

hints.rationale: Total energy = the kinetic energies plus the elastic PE.

hints.prompt: The compression is Δx = 1.0 − 0.80 = 0.20 m, and each 4.0-kg mass moves at 0.50 m/s. Which expression gives the total energy?
hints.rationale: Elastic PE uses the 0.20 m compression, and BOTH masses contribute kinetic energy.

hints.prompt: The spring's COMPRESSION is 1.0 − 0.80 = 0.20 m, not 0.80 m. Options that use 0.80 m for the spring energy (giving ~8–9 J) are traps. Which pair can be eliminated?
hints.rationale: Using 0.80 m instead of the 0.20 m compression overstates the elastic energy (~8–9 J), so both are eliminated.

questions.stem: You are loading a toy dart gun, which has two settings; the more powerful setting compresses the spring twice as far as the lower setting. If it takes 5.0 J of work to compress the dart gun to the lower setting, how much work does it take for the higher setting?
questions.explanation: The work stored in a spring is ½kx², proportional to the square of the compression. Doubling the compression multiplies the work by 4, so 5.0 J × 4 = 20 J.
questions.sourceName: OpenStax College Physics for AP® Courses 2e, Ch. 7 — Test Prep for AP® Courses
questions.sourceLicense: CC BY-NC-SA 4.0 (OpenStax; live page also posts a no-LLM-training notice — SpeedyCAT use is human-facing, non-AI per PRD)
questions.notes: Stem + 4 options scraped verbatim. OpenStax withholds the public answer key; answer independently verified (spring energy ∝ compression²).

hints.prompt: The work stored in a compressed spring is:

choices.label: A
choices.text: proportional to the square of the compression (½kx²)

hints.rationale: Spring energy is ½kx², proportional to x².

hints.prompt: Compressing twice as far multiplies the stored work by what factor?
hints.rationale: Work ∝ x², so doubling x multiplies the work by 4.

hints.prompt: Compressing farther takes MORE than 5.0 J (eliminating anything smaller), but the factor is 2² = 4, not 2³ = 8. Which pair of options can be eliminated?
hints.rationale: 2.5 J is less than the original 5.0 J (impossible) and 40 J uses a factor of 8 instead of 4, so both are eliminated.

questions.stem: Old-fashioned pendulum clocks are powered by masses that are wound back to the top of the clock about once a week. One particular clock has three masses: 4.0 kg, 4.0 kg, and 6.0 kg, which can drop 1.3 meters. About how much energy does the clock use in a week? (Take g = 9.8 m/s².)
questions.explanation: The energy is the total gravitational potential energy released as the masses drop: PE = mgh = (4.0 + 4.0 + 6.0) kg × 9.8 m/s² × 1.3 m = 14 × 9.8 × 1.3 ≈ 178 J.
questions.sourceName: OpenStax College Physics for AP® Courses 2e, Ch. 7 — Test Prep for AP® Courses
questions.sourceLicense: CC BY-NC-SA 4.0 (OpenStax; live page also posts a no-LLM-training notice — SpeedyCAT use is human-facing, non-AI per PRD)
questions.notes: Stem + 4 options scraped verbatim. OpenStax withholds the public answer key; answer independently verified by calculation (total mgh).

hints.prompt: The gravitational potential energy released by a falling mass is:
hints.rationale: Energy released equals mgh.

hints.prompt: For three masses (4.0, 4.0, 6.0 kg) each dropping 1.3 m, the total energy is:
hints.rationale: Add the masses (14 kg total) and use mgh with g and the 1.3 m drop.

hints.prompt: All THREE masses (total 14 kg) drop, so the energy must reflect the full 14 kg—not just one mass. Which pair of options (each based on too little mass) can be eliminated?
hints.rationale: 51 J and 76 J correspond to only a single 4.0-kg or 6.0-kg mass, far short of the full 14 kg, so both are eliminated.

questions.stem: Old-fashioned pocket watches needed to be wound daily. Winding required a large number of turns of the key but not much force per turn, and it was possible to overwind and break the watch. How was the energy stored?

choices.label: A
choices.text: A small mass raised a long distance

choices.label: B
choices.text: A large mass raised a short distance

choices.label: C
choices.text: A weak spring deformed a long way

choices.label: D
choices.text: A strong spring deformed a short way

questions.explanation: A watch stores energy in a coiled mainspring. The many turns required correspond to a large deformation, and the small force per turn indicates a relatively weak spring—so the energy is stored as a weak spring deformed a long way.
questions.sourceName: OpenStax College Physics for AP® Courses 2e, Ch. 7 — Test Prep for AP® Courses
questions.sourceLicense: CC BY-NC-SA 4.0 (OpenStax; live page also posts a no-LLM-training notice — SpeedyCAT use is human-facing, non-AI per PRD)
questions.notes: Stem + 4 options scraped verbatim. OpenStax withholds the public answer key; answer independently verified from the description (many low-force turns = weak spring, large deformation).

hints.prompt: A wound watch stores energy in which form?

choices.label: A
choices.text: elastic potential energy in a coiled spring (the mainspring)

choices.label: B
choices.text: kinetic energy of a spinning flywheel

choices.label: C
choices.text: gravitational PE of a raised mass

hints.rationale: A watch stores energy in the elastic PE of its coiled mainspring.

hints.prompt: Winding took MANY turns (a large deformation) but only a SMALL force per turn. What does 'small force per turn' say about the spring's stiffness?

choices.label: A
choices.text: the spring is relatively weak (low stiffness)

choices.label: B
choices.text: the spring is very strong (high stiffness)

hints.rationale: A small force for each turn indicates a weak (low-stiffness) spring.

hints.prompt: A pocket watch stores energy in a spring, not by raising a mass. Which pair of options can be eliminated?

choices.label: A
choices.text: 'A small mass raised a long distance' and 'A large mass raised a short distance'

choices.label: B
choices.text: 'A weak spring deformed a long way' and 'A strong spring deformed a short way'

choices.label: C
choices.text: 'A small mass raised a long distance' and 'A weak spring deformed a long way'

choices.label: D
choices.text: 'A large mass raised a short distance' and 'A strong spring deformed a short way'

hints.rationale: The watch uses a spring, so both mass-raising options are eliminated.

questions.stem: You are in a basement with a smooth concrete floor (friction force 40 N) and a rug (friction force 55 N) that is 3 m by 4 m. You must push a very heavy box from one corner of the rug to the opposite corner. Will you do more work against friction going around the edge on the floor or straight across the rug, and how much extra?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Across the rug is 275 J extra

choices.label: B
choices.text: Around the floor is 5 J extra

choices.label: C
choices.text: Across the rug is 5 J extra

choices.label: D
choices.text: Around the floor is 280 J extra

questions.explanation: Across the rug (the diagonal) the distance is √(3² + 4²) = 5 m, so work = 55 N × 5 m = 275 J. Around the floor along two edges is 3 + 4 = 7 m, so work = 40 N × 7 m = 280 J. Going around the floor takes 280 - 275 = 5 J more.
questions.sourceName: OpenStax College Physics for AP® Courses 2e, Ch. 7 — Test Prep for AP® Courses
questions.sourceLicense: CC BY-NC-SA 4.0 (OpenStax; live page also posts a no-LLM-training notice — SpeedyCAT use is human-facing, non-AI per PRD)
questions.notes: Stem + 4 options scraped verbatim. OpenStax withholds the public answer key; answer independently verified by calculation (path length × friction force).

hints.prompt: The work done against a constant friction force along a path is:

choices.label: A
choices.text: the friction force times the path length

choices.label: B
choices.text: the friction force divided by the path length

choices.label: D
choices.text: the mass times the path length

hints.rationale: Work against friction = friction force × distance traveled.

hints.prompt: The rug diagonal is √(3²+4²) = 5 m (friction 55 N); the floor route along two edges is 3+4 = 7 m (friction 40 N). Which two products do you compare?
hints.rationale: Multiply each path's friction force by that path's length: rug 55×5, floor 40×7.

hints.prompt: The two amounts of work are about 275 J and 280 J, so the DIFFERENCE ('extra') is only about 5 J—not 275 or 280 J. Which pair of options can be eliminated for citing the wrong 'extra'?

choices.label: A
choices.text: 'Across the rug is 275 J extra' and 'Around the floor is 280 J extra'

choices.label: B
choices.text: 'Around the floor is 5 J extra' and 'Across the rug is 5 J extra'

choices.label: C
choices.text: 'Across the rug is 275 J extra' and 'Around the floor is 5 J extra'

choices.label: D
choices.text: 'Around the floor is 280 J extra' and 'Across the rug is 5 J extra'

hints.rationale: 275 J and 280 J are the total works, not the extra; the extra is their ~5 J difference, so those two options are eliminated.

questions.stem: You do 30 J of work to load a toy dart gun. However, the dart is 10 cm long and feels a frictional force of 10 N while going through the dart gun's barrel. What is the kinetic energy of the fired dart?
questions.explanation: Friction does negative work over the barrel length: W_friction = 10 N × 0.10 m = 1 J. By energy conservation, KE = 30 J - 1 J = 29 J.
questions.sourceName: OpenStax College Physics for AP® Courses 2e, Ch. 7 — Test Prep for AP® Courses
questions.sourceLicense: CC BY-NC-SA 4.0 (OpenStax; live page also posts a no-LLM-training notice — SpeedyCAT use is human-facing, non-AI per PRD)
questions.notes: Stem + 4 options scraped verbatim. OpenStax withholds the public answer key; answer independently verified (work in minus friction work).

hints.prompt: When friction acts over a distance, the kinetic energy delivered is the input energy:

choices.label: A
choices.text: minus the work done by friction (force × distance)

choices.label: B
choices.text: plus the work done by friction

hints.rationale: Friction removes energy, so KE = input energy − friction work.

hints.prompt: The barrel/dart length is 10 cm = 0.10 m and friction is 10 N. How much energy does friction remove?
hints.rationale: Friction work = 10 N × 0.10 m = 1 J (note 10 cm = 0.10 m).

hints.prompt: Friction removes exactly 10 N × 0.10 m = 1 J, so the kinetic energy is 1 J below 30 J—not 0 J below and not 3 J below. Which pair of options can be eliminated?
hints.rationale: A 1 J loss rules out 30 J (no loss) and 27 J (a 3 J loss), so both are eliminated.

questions.stem: A child has two red wagons, with the rear one tied to the front by a stretchy rope (which acts like a spring). If the child pulls on the front wagon, the ____ increases.

choices.label: B
choices.text: potential energy stored in the spring

questions.explanation: Pulling the front wagon speeds the wagons up (kinetic energy increases) and stretches the elastic rope as the front wagon pulls ahead of the rear one (elastic potential energy increases). Both forms of energy increase.
questions.sourceName: OpenStax College Physics for AP® Courses 2e, Ch. 7 — Test Prep for AP® Courses

questions.notes: Stem + 4 options scraped verbatim. OpenStax withholds the public answer key; answer independently verified.

hints.prompt: A stretchy rope (acting like a spring) stores potential energy when it is:

choices.label: A
choices.text: stretched (its length increases beyond natural length)

hints.rationale: An elastic rope stores PE when stretched from its natural length.

hints.prompt: As the pulled front wagon momentarily pulls AHEAD of the rear wagon, what happens to the stretchy rope between them?

choices.label: A
choices.text: it stretches, storing elastic potential energy

choices.label: D
choices.text: it stays exactly at its natural length

hints.rationale: The front wagon moving ahead stretches the elastic rope, storing PE.

hints.prompt: The scenario is fully specified, and BOTH speeding up and stretching occur. Which pair of options can be eliminated?

choices.label: A
choices.text: 'kinetic energy of the wagons (only)' and 'not enough information'

choices.label: B
choices.text: 'potential energy stored in the spring (only)' and 'both A and B'

choices.label: C
choices.text: 'kinetic energy of the wagons (only)' and 'both A and B'

choices.label: D
choices.text: 'not enough information' and 'both A and B'

hints.rationale: The scenario is fully specified (eliminating 'not enough information') and more than just the wagons' KE changes (eliminating the KE-only option).

questions.stem: A 5.0-kg rock falls off of a 10 m cliff. If air resistance exerts an average force of 10 N opposing the motion, what is the kinetic energy when the rock hits the ground? (Use g = 10 m/s².)
questions.explanation: Energy released by gravity = mgh = 5.0 × 10 × 10 = 500 J. Air resistance removes W = 10 N × 10 m = 100 J. So KE at the ground = 500 - 100 = 400 J. (Option B is wrong because it is a speed, not an energy.)
questions.sourceName: OpenStax College Physics for AP® Courses 2e, Ch. 7 — Test Prep for AP® Courses
questions.sourceLicense: CC BY-NC-SA 4.0 (OpenStax; live page also posts a no-LLM-training notice — SpeedyCAT use is human-facing, non-AI per PRD)
questions.notes: Stem + 4 options scraped verbatim. OpenStax withholds the public answer key; answer independently verified by calculation. The answer choices imply g = 10 m/s² (mgh - friction work = 500 - 100 = 400 J exactly).

hints.prompt: When an object falls WITH air resistance, its kinetic energy at the bottom equals:

choices.label: A
choices.text: the gravitational energy released (mgh) minus the energy removed by air resistance

hints.rationale: KE = energy released by gravity (mgh) minus the work done against air resistance.

hints.prompt: Gravity releases mgh = 5.0×10×10 and air resistance removes 10 N × 10 m. Which combination gives the KE at the ground?
hints.rationale: Subtract the air-resistance work (100 J) from the gravitational energy (500 J).

hints.prompt: The answer must be an ENERGY in joules (so a value in m/s is wrong), and air resistance definitely removes some energy (so it cannot be the full 500 J). Which pair of options can be eliminated?
hints.rationale: '12.6 m/s' has the wrong units for energy, and 500 J ignores the air-resistance loss, so both are eliminated.

questions.stem: Before railroads, goods often traveled along canals, with mules pulling barges from the bank. If a mule exerts a 1200 N force for 10 km, and the rope connecting the mule to the barge is at a 20 degree angle from the direction of travel, how much work did the mule do on the barge?
questions.explanation: Work = F·d·cosθ = 1200 N × 10,000 m × cos(20°) = 1.2 × 10⁷ × 0.940 ≈ 1.1 × 10⁷ J ≈ 11 MJ. Only the component of force along the direction of travel does work.
questions.sourceName: OpenStax College Physics for AP® Courses 2e, Ch. 7 — Test Prep for AP® Courses
questions.sourceLicense: CC BY-NC-SA 4.0 (OpenStax; live page also posts a no-LLM-training notice — SpeedyCAT use is human-facing, non-AI per PRD)
questions.notes: Stem + 4 options scraped verbatim. OpenStax withholds the public answer key; answer independently verified by calculation (W = F d cosθ).

hints.prompt: When a force acts at an angle θ to the direction of motion, the work it does is:

choices.label: A
choices.text: W = F·d·cosθ (only the component along the motion)

hints.rationale: Only the force component along the displacement does work: W = Fd cosθ.

hints.prompt: Here F = 1200 N, d = 10 km = 10,000 m, θ = 20° (cos 20° ≈ 0.94). Which setup is correct?
hints.rationale: Use W = Fd cosθ with d in meters (10 km = 10,000 m).

hints.prompt: Including cos 20° (≈0.94) reduces the work only slightly below the 12 MJ you'd get ignoring the angle—and you must use cos, not sin. Which pair of options can be eliminated?
hints.rationale: 12 MJ ignores the angle entirely and 4.1 MJ uses sin 20° instead of cos, so both are eliminated.
